steps.prompt: Is there a finite verb in this sentence?

hints.Yes — 'Timor' is a verb: 'Timor' is a noun meaning 'fear' — it is the subject of this sentence
hints.Yes — 'principium' is a verb: 'principium' is a noun meaning 'beginning' — it is the predicate nominative
hints.Yes — 'sapientiae' is a verb: 'sapientiae' is a noun in the genitive case meaning 'of wisdom'

hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'timor' would be 'timoris' — 'Timor' is the base nominative form
hints.Accusative: Accusative of 'timor' would be 'timorem' — 'Timor' lacks the -em ending
hints.Ablative: Ablative of 'timor' would be 'timore' — 'Timor' is the nominative singular

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns typically end in -a (e.g., 'puella') — 'timor' has genitive 'timoris'
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension nouns typically end in -us or -um — 'timor' has genitive 'timoris'
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'timor' has genitive 'timoris' with the -is ending

hints.Nominative: Nominative of 'Dominus' is 'Dominus' — 'Domini' has the -i genitive ending
hints.Dative: Dative of 'Dominus' would be 'Domino' — 'Domini' ends in -i, the genitive singular
hints.Vocative: Vocative of 'Dominus' would be 'Domine' — 'Domini' ends in -i, the genitive singular

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'Domini' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'Domini' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'Domini' ends in -i

hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'principium' would be 'principii' — 'principium' is the nominative/accusative form
hints.Accusative: While 'principium' could be accusative, here it serves as the predicate nominative with the implied 'est'
hints.Ablative: Ablative of 'principium' would be 'principio' — 'principium' retains the -um ending

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'principii' ends in -ii
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'principii' ends in -ii
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'principii' ends in -ii

hints.Nominative: Nominative of 'sapientia' is 'sapientia' — 'sapientiae' has the -ae ending indicating genitive or dative
hints.Dative: While 'sapientiae' could be dative in form, here it means 'of wisdom' modifying 'principium'
hints.Ablative: Ablative of 'sapientia' would be 'sapientiā' — 'sapientiae' has the -ae genitive ending

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension nouns have genitive in -i — 'sapientiae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'sapientiae' ends in -ae
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'sapientiae' ends in -ae

translation: The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom
